msgs.text:
Turn 22 — coefficient-lift audit: Turn 21 still needs a stronger nonemptiness lemma I re-read only the three files attached to this message. The problem is still the over-G isomorphism question for the two quotients ΓS,ΓH, where F=P⋊G, P is the free G-operator pro-2 group on x0,x1, and rS,rH are the relators in the problem statement. The requested conclusion is an isomorphism ΓS→ΓH satisfying πH∘Φ=πS.The main result of this turn is a tightening, not a new obstruction:

Turn 21’s compactness framework is structurally right, but its nonemptiness claim still needs a coefficient-Hensel proof.

Turn 21 correctly fixed Turn 20’s false finite-quotient statement. Degree-only quotients P/PN are generally not finite because the first graded layer already carries Λ=Z2 [[G]]-coefficients. The repaired system used coefficient-and-degree truncationsMN,J=M/(IN+JM),PN,J=P∩(1+IN+JM),so P/PN,J is finite and the inverse-limit compactness argument can run over the directed pairs (N,J).The compactness part itself is sound: if every finite solution set SN,J is nonempty, then the finite-intersection argument produces a compatible family, hence an over-G automorphism of F carrying rS±1 to rH, and therefore an over-G isomorphism ΓS≅GΓH.

The remaining stress point is the sentenceSN,J=∅for every N,J.

Turn 21 justified this by saying that the non-residual pieces are unit-controlled and that Turn 18 killed the residual Ef, mod-2, s=1 obstruction tower. That is morally the right mechanism, but it is not yet a complete proof over arbitrary finite coefficient quotients Λ/J. Turn 18 proves the residual all-order statementCm=Lm (m≥5),so every even square quotient is hit by a root transgression and every odd layer is an ordinary boundary in the residual mod-2, s=1 restricted Magnus problem.

But SN,J=∅ asks for solvability over every finite coefficient quotient, not only over the residue field of the Ef-local coefficient ring.The missing lemma should be stated explicitly.LetΛ=Z2 [[G]],E=e⟨τ⟩,f=e⟨a⟩,a=σ 1−π2,s=σ2.After the Turn 1 abelianized change of variables, the coefficient algebra splits into (1−E)Λ⊕E(1−f)Λ⊕EfΛ.On the first two summands, the linearized correction operator has a unit coefficient; this is the unit-piece argument already used throughout the sweep. On the Ef-summand, the coefficient algebra is the local pro-2 piece, with residue obtained by imposing 2=0 and s=1. That residue is exactly the residual Magnus problem solved in Turn 18. The abelianized matrix U carrying wS to wH, including the Ef-piece matrix with determinant s, was already constructed in Turn 1.

So the needed coefficient-Hensel lemma is:Coefficient-Hensel lemma needed.For every finite Artinian quotient A of the Ef-coefficient ring and every finite Magnus degree N, the residual all-order solution overA/mA≃F2 lifts to a solution over A, after allowing the same lower-plus-high torsor and section cocycle freedoms used in Turns 12–18.This is not a cosmetic detail. The degree-16 episode shows why. In a fixed high gauge, the square quotient had a genuine codimension-one miss; it disappeared only after retuning the lower I9 -base by the Y0↦Y0Y4 deformation.That mechanism is nonlinear and Bockstein-sensitive. Therefore one cannot replace the coefficient-Hensel lemma by the naive assertion “residual solvability implies coefficient solvability” without proving formal smoothness, or at least proving the appropriate finite Artinian lifting statement.The right structural repair is to define the finite solution problem over each local coefficient quotient A as a finite system in all lower and higher correction variables at once. Then prove by induction on the length of A that the reduction mapSN (A)⟶SN (A/a)is surjective for every square-zero ideal a⊂A. At a square-zero extension, all new error terms are linear in a. The non-Ef summands are solved by unit coefficients. On the Ef summand, Nakayama reduces the obstruction map to the residual restricted-Lie complex, and Turn 18’s statement Cm=Lm supplies exactly the missing square-root directions. If this lemma is proved, Turn 21’s SN,J=∅ follows for every (N,J), and the compactness proof is complete.

So the conclusion remains strongly pointed towardΓS≅GΓH,but Turn 22 downgrades the status from “fully closed” to “closed modulo one precise coefficient-lifting lemma.” The remaining issue is not another isolated Magnus case; it is a uniform Artinian lifting theorem that turns the residual Ef induction into finite coefficient-level nonemptiness.

ESTABLISHED: Turn 21’s corrected compactness framework is the right global inverse-limit framework: use coefficient-and-degree quotients P/PN,J, finite solution sets SN,J, and compactness. The residual obstruction tower over the Ef residue field is already closed by Cm=Lm for all m≥5.

GAP: The implication residual Ef solvability⟹SN,J=∅ for every finite coefficient quotient J has not yet been fully proved. It needs a coefficient-Hensel / square-zero Artinian lifting lemma that includes the lower-base torsor, Bockstein terms, and section cocycle.

NEXT: Prove the coefficient-Hensel lemma by induction over square-zero extensions of finite quotients of EfΛ. Once that is written, Turn 21’s compactness argument becomes a complete proof of ΓS≅GΓH.[TURN22-COEFFICIENT-HENSEL-GAP]
